summary: My grandfather, Oliver O'Mahoney, was born in Ireland on July 3rd, 1938. He grew up in Dundalk, where his father was the chief of police. Ireland is where his roots are, and although he moved to America in the early 1960s, he always carried a connection to his homeland. His journey from Ireland to the US Army and eventually to America shaped much of our family's story.

My grandmother, Julia O'Mahoney, was born in Manila, Philippines in 1939. It was in the Philippines where she met my grandfather while he was stationed there with the US Army preparing for the Vietnam War. Manila was her home before she moved to the United States for a student nurse exchange program. Her journey from Manila to America was influenced by family circumstances and her desire to pursue nursing.

The Philippines is where my grandmother was born and raised, specifically in Manila. My grandfather was sent there with the US Army before the Vietnam War, and that's where they met. After some family challenges, including the illness and passing of my grandmother’s father, she decided to move to the US for a two-year student nurse exchange program. The Philippines played a crucial role as the starting point of her journey to America.

My grandfather enlisted in the United States Army after his father passed away. He was sent to the Philippines to help prepare for the Vietnam War, which is where he met my grandmother. The Army was a significant part of his life, and it also influenced our family’s path, including his efforts to transfer to California and later New Jersey. The Army also helped him gain citizenship faster, and it was an experience he valued deeply.

The Vietnam War was the backdrop for my grandfather’s military service. He was sent to the Philippines as part of the US Army’s preparation for the conflict. This period was pivotal because it led to him meeting my grandmother. The war indirectly shaped our family’s future by bringing them together during those uncertain times.

California was the place my grandfather initially thought he would be transferred to after the Philippines. He told my grandmother to move there, hoping they would be together. However, he was first stationed in Hawaii and later found himself transferred to New Jersey instead. Despite the mix-up, California was important as a hopeful destination and a connection point in their plans.
cues: initial plan for grandmother to move
later stationed in Hawaii and New Jersey

summary: After leaving the Philippines, my grandfather was stationed in Hawaii for several months. He was eager to be transferred to California, so he kept submitting requests each week. Hawaii was a temporary stop in his military service, but it was a significant part of his journey before eventually moving to New Jersey. It was during this time that my grandmother was still in the Philippines considering her move to the US.

My grandmother joined a student nurse exchange program that brought her from the Philippines to the United States. This two-year program allowed her to work in hospitals, first in Chicago with her sister and then in Newark, New Jersey. The program was delayed due to her father's illness but became a turning point for her career and life in America. It also reunited her geographically with my grandfather after his transfer to New Jersey.

When my grandmother first came to the United States through the student nurse exchange program, she lived in Chicago with her eldest sister. Chicago was her initial American home before she was placed to work in a hospital in Newark, New Jersey. It was an important transitional city for her as she adjusted to life in the US and prepared for her nursing career.

Newark, New Jersey became a key place for both my grandparents. My grandmother worked in a hospital there as part of her nursing exchange program. My grandfather was transferred there unexpectedly by the Army, despite his hopes to be in California. Ironically, this brought them close together again, living just 30 minutes apart and able to reconnect after their time apart.

After his military service, my grandfather worked for the Campbell Soup Company while studying to earn his pilot’s license. He spent about four years there before leaving to sell airplanes both domestically and internationally. Campbell Soup was an important part of his post-military career and helped him transition into civilian life and new professional opportunities.
